coredata.dc:title: Constructing three-dimensional porous graphene-carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheet aerogel metal-free photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: He, Huijuan
Huang, Langhuan
Zhong, Zijun
Tan, Shaozao
coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis has been widely considered to be an effective way for solving the worldwide environmental pollution issues. Herein, a new type of three-dimensional (3D) ternary graphene-carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheet (GA-CQDs/CNN) aerogel visible-light-driven photocatalyst was synthesized via a two-step hydrothermal method. In this unique ternary photocatalyst, both carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) could improve the visible light absorption and promote the charge separation. Furthermore, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) could act as a supportor for the 3D framework. Such a ternary system overcame the drawbacks of bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) and achieved the enhanced photocatalytic activity and long-term stability. As a result, the methyl orange (MO) removal ratio of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was up to 91.1%, which was about 7.6 times higher than that of bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) under the identical conditions. Moreover that GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibited inappreciable loss of photocatalytic activity after four-cycle degradation processes. Finally, the photocatalytic mechanism of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was interpreted both theoretically and experimentally.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
Visible-light
g-C3N4, graphene
Carbon quantum dots
Three-dimensional aerogel

National Natural Science Foundation of China – China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Plan Project of Guangdong Province China [ 2016A010103047 ] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China – China [ 21476052 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) BCN, CNN, GO, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24%, (b) rGO and GA-CQDs/CNN with different GO content.
FTIR spectra of rGO, CNN, BCN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24%.
XPS spectra of BCN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24%: (a) Survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s.

FESEM image of (a) CNN, (b) Low- and (c) high-magnification FESEM images of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%, (d) FESEM image and (e–f) corresponding elemental mappings of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. (Inset: a partial enlargement of in this figure a.)
(a) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for BCN, CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24%, respectively, (b) BJH pore-size distributions for GA-CQDs/CNN-24%.
The TEM image of (a) CQDs, (b) GO, (c) CNN and (d) CQDs/CNN. (e) Low- (f) high-magnification TEM and (g) HRTEM images of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%.
(a) UV-vis absorption spectra, (b) PL emission spectra of BCN, CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. Inset: (ahν)2 versus hν curve.
(a) Transient photocurrent density versus time plots, (b) EIS Nyquist plots of the BCN, CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24%.
Absorption capacity of as-prepared photocatalysts on MO aqueous solution within 90 min.

(a) Absorptive, photocatalytic removal of aqueous MO over as-prepared photocatalysts under visible light (λ > 420 nm), (b) corresponding kinetic simulation curves and (c) cycle runs of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% for MO removal.

ESR spectra of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% before and after visible light irradiation: (a) DMPO-
O2 − in methanol dispersion (b) DMPO-
OH in aqueous dispersion and (c) h+ in powder.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of GA-CQDs/CNN.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic activity of GA-CQDs/CNN.

head.ce:title: Constructing three-dimensional porous graphene-carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheet aerogel metal-free photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, NO. 601, The West of Huangpu Street, Guangzhou 510632, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis has been widely considered to be an effective way for solving the worldwide environmental pollution issues. Herein, a new type of three-dimensional (3D) ternary graphene-carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 nanosheet (GA-CQDs/CNN) aerogel visible-light-driven photocatalyst was synthesized via a two-step hydrothermal method. In this unique ternary photocatalyst, both carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) could improve the visible light absorption and promote the charge separation. Furthermore, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) could act as a supportor for the 3D framework. Such a ternary system overcame the drawbacks of bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) and achieved the enhanced photocatalytic activity and long-term stability. As a result, the methyl orange (MO) removal ratio of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was up to 91.1%, which was about 7.6 times higher than that of bulk g-C3N4 (BCN) under the identical conditions. Moreover that GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibited inappreciable loss of photocatalytic activity after four-cycle degradation processes. Finally, the photocatalytic mechanism of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was interpreted both theoretically and experimentally.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic removal of organic pollutant with inexhaustible solar energy has already been widely researched for solving the growing pollution issues [1–4]. Developing an active and stable visible-light responsive photocatalyst is crucial for practical applications of photocatalysis technology. In the past few decades, various photocatalysts had been developed [5–9]. Most of these photocatalysts, however, generally suffered from poor visible light activity. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as a metal-free polymeric semi-conducting material, has become a very hot research topic since Wang et al. first reported that it has excellent photocatalytic ability in 2009 [10–13]. And g-C3N4 has been recognized as a novel photocatalyst in multifaceted applications due to its excellent chemical and thermal stability, appropriate band structures, low-cost and environmentally friendly features [14–17]. However, the photocatalytic performance of bulk g-C3N4 was severely limited by its insufficient solar-light absorption, low specific surface area, and fast recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes which caused by π–π conjugated electronic system [18–20].

To improve the photocatalytic activity of g-C3N4, many strategies have been carried out. They mainly include developing various nanostructures (such as nanosheets, nanoparticles, nanorods and nanospheres) [21–25], constructing heterojunctions with other semiconductor materials [26–32], modifying with vacancies and decorating g-C3N4 with metal nanoparticles or carbon nanomaterials [33–37]. In particular, it have been proved decorating g-C3N4 nanosheet with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could facilitate the separation of photoinduced electron–hole efficiently, because the π-π stacking interactions between CQDs and CNN could suppress the charge recombination greatly by forming a type-II van der Waals heterojunction [38–43]. However, the reported g-C3N4/CQDs were still unsuitable for commercial applications owing to their nanoscale morphology.

As a star material, three-dimensional (3D) graphene aerogel (GA) has got more and more attentions due to its hierarchically porous structure and large accessible surface area, which can act as an ideal support for the deposition of photocatalyst nanoparticles and improve the photocatalytic performance by facilitating adsorption and photoreaction [44–49]. Meanwhile, compared with 2D graphene, its macroscopic block appearance is favorable for recycling operation. Moreover, the densities, shapes, and volumes of 3D GA can be easily adjusted by changing the concentration of reactants and the size of reaction vessel, which portends the possibility of large production for commercial applications. It is noteworthy that most of those reported 3D GA photocatalysts were limited to binary composite materials, which was still far from reaching expected photocatalytic activity. To the best of our knowledge, there was no study on a ternary 3D intercorrelated photocatalyst composed by g-C3N4, CQDs and reduced graphene oxide (rGO).

Herein, we constructed a ternary 3D porous aerogel photocatalyst with rGO, CQDs and CNN for the first time. The resultant GA-CQDs/CNN possessed a 3D interconnected network. With such a unique structure, GA-CQDs/CNN had good structural stability, large specific surface area, lightweigh and freestanding performance. Remarkably, compared with single g-C3N4, the distinctive GA-CQDs/CNN ternary aerogel photocatalyst exhibited excellent charge separation efficiency and visible light absorption capacity. Resultly, it possessed significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance for degradation of MB and MO under visible light.

Scheme 1 illustrated the fabrication scheme and the corresponding digital photograph of the 3D porous GA-CQDs/CNN. Such a ternary composite was fabricated by a two-step hydrothermal method. Firstly, CQDs/CNN was fabricated through the first-step hydrothermal process, where CQDs was loaded on CNN tightly by π-π stacking interactions. Subsequently, CQDs/CNN was mixed with oxidized graphene (GO) and then the mixture was placed in an autoclave for the second hydrothermal process, where the reduction of GO and the formation of a ternary composite was achieved simultaneously. The resulting product was washed with distilled water and freeze-dried. The obtained sample was designated as GA-CQDs/CNN. A series of GA-CQDs/CNN photocatalysts were prepared by changing the mass ratio of GO to CNN (mGO:mCNN = 8%, 16%, 24%, 32%, and 40% respectively) and marked as e.g. GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. The details of the preparation procedure were described in the Supporting Information.

The crystal structures of BCN, CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As presented in Fig. 1 a, two distinct peaks were observed in the diffraction patterns of BCN. The strong peak at 27.85° was correspond to the interlayer stacking of aromatic systems, indexed as (0 0 2) plane. And the weak peak at 13.29° was in-plane structural packing of tri-s-triazine units and indexed as (1 0 0) plane [13]. As for CNN, obvious decrease of the intensity of (0 0 2) peak and the absence of (1 0 0) peak was observed, which suggested the layered g-C3N4 had been successfully exfoliated as we expected [50]. For pure GO, only a sharp diffraction peak appeared at around 10.13°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) reflection [51]. Noticeably both the X-ray diffraction patterns of CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24% almost coincided with that of pure CNN, namely the peaks originated from CQDs were not observed. It was possibly due to the low content and the low diffraction intensity of CQDs. However, the presence of CQDs could be discerned by TEM as discussed later. As shown in Fig. 1a and b, the intensity of (0 0 2) diffraction peak of CNN in as-prepared GA-CQDs/CNN became weaker and wider gradually with the increase of GO content. It indicated that the ordered crystalline structure of carbon nitride was changed, which might be attributed to the strong coupling between g-C3N4 layers and rGO nanosheets formed in hydrothermal process.

As shown in Fig. 2 , the FTIR spectrum of rGO exhibited three weak peaks at 1384, 1630 and 1082 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of CO, CC and COC, respectively [52]. In the case of pure g-C3N4, the peaks at 1240–1640 cm−1 were related to the typical stretching modes of CN heterocycles, and the broad band centered at 3000–3500 cm−1 could be assigned to the stretching mode of N–H, while the peak at 809 cm−1 was ascribed to the characteristic breathing mode of tri-s-triazine unites in g-C3N4 [53,54]. Clearly, there were no obvious differences among the FT-IR spectra of BCN, CNN and CQDs/CNN, which indicated that the exfoliated nanosheets kept the same chemical structure as that of their parent bulk g-C3N4 [55]. It was worth noting that most of the above characteristic peaks were appeared in the spectrum of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%, which suggested that rGO and CQDs/CNN combinated successfully. This could be confirmed by TEM images discussed later. Particularly, the FTIR spectrum of GA-CQDs/CNN displayed an obvious change compared to those of BCN, CNN and CQDs/CNN, which might be attributed to the formation of covalent bonds between GO and CNN.

To further investigate the chemical structure of the as-prepared samples, the XPS measurements were conducted. In Fig. 3 a, the XPS survey spectrum of BCN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24% only displayed the presence of C, N and O elements. In Fig. 3b, the C1s spectrum of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% could be deconvoluted into three peaks. The dominate peak at 284.2 eV was attributed to the graphitic carbon (CC), which became much stronger compared with that of BCN. This was due to the introduction of rGO and CQDs. The peak at 288.0 eV originated from sp2 C atoms of NCN inside the aromatic structure. Furthermore, the peak at 285.4 eV was attributed to the C-O. The N1s spectrum of both BCN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibited two main peaks at 398.4 eV and 399.7 eV, which could be assigned to the nitrogen of CNC and tertiary nitrogen N(C)3 in heptazine unit, respectively. Meanwhile, Fig. 3d represented the O1s peak at 531.6 eV which was assigned to the adsorbed H2O on the catalyst surface for BCN. While for GA-CQDs/CNN-24%, except for H2O on its surface, the peak at 530.9 eV was attributed to the residual C-O bond from rGO [47]. Remarkably, as shown in Fig. 3b and c, the binding energy of NCN in C 1s and CNC in N 1 s in GA-CQDs/CNN-24% had obvious shift when compared with that of BCN. This could be attributed to the decrease of charge density around CNN due to the introduction of highly conductive rGO [46].

The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) study showed that the bare CNN was fluffy and consisted of many curved nanosheets with lamellar morphology (Fig. 4 a). The inset in Fig. 4a was a magnified FESEM image of CNN, in which we could find that the nanosheets with a thickness of about 50 nm were in an irregular shape. In Fig. 4b, the 3D porous structure of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was readily identified. As shown, the 3D interconnected network stacked by CNN and rGO possessed a porous, wrinkled and fluffy microstructure. And the enlarged image in Fig. 4c obviously revealed that the hierarchical pores were highly interconnected and the diameter was in the range of 50–500 nm. Besides macropores, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% also contained mesopores with the pore size mainly lying in the range of 3–5 nm (Fig. 5 b). Owing to the high interconnection and overlapping of the curved CNN and rGO nanosheets, it was difficult to distinguish between the two of them in the FESEM image. While as shown in the element mapping, C (Fig. 4e), and N (Fig. 4f) existed homogeneously within the selected area of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% (Fig. 4d). This demonstrated that CNN distributed homogeneously in GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. Additionally, the microtopographies of the obtained GA-CQDs/CNN with different GO content were also characterized. As shown in Fig. S1, the content of GO had a great influence on both micro and macro morphology of GA-CQDs/CNN. The mechanical properties increased when the mass ratio of GO to CNN changed from 8% to 40%. Meanwhile, the specific surface area and macropore size of GA-CQDs/CNN could be easily adjusted by changing the content of GO.

More detailed information about the framework of the samples were accessed by nitrogen absorption–desorption isotherms. As shown in Fig. 5a, BCN showed isotherms of typeⅡ, while the isotherms of CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA- CQDs/CNN-24% were type IV according to the Brunauer-Deming-Deming-Teller (BDDT) classification, indicating the porous property of CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA- CQDs/CNN. Particularly, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% showed a high adsorption in the high-relative-pressure (P/P0 = 0.8–1.0) region. This finding indicated the existence of mesopores and macropores. Correspondingly, the Brunauer‐Emmett‐Teller (BET) surface areas of CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24% were larger than that of BCN because of their porous microstructures. In addition, the BET surface area of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was larger than those of CNN and CQDs/CNN because of its 3D porous structure formed by GO. The existence of mesopores was also confirmed by the pore-size distributions of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%, as shown in Fig. 5b, which indicates that the pore size mainly lied in the range of 3–5 nm owning to the defect hole in the CNN.

Fig. 6 a displayed the TEM image of pure CQDs. As shown, the CQDs with diameters of 5–10 nm were well dispersed. In Fig. 6b and c, both GO and CNN exhibited two-dimensional laminated structure. Particularly, compared with CNN, GO nanosheet was much larger and thinner, which might be helpful for building the 3D framework. In Fig. 6d, CQDs loaded on the surfaces of CNN was observed, and the size of them was in accordance with that of pure CQDs. In Fig. 6e and Fig. 6f, graphene substrate was covered by CQDs/CNN and the as prepared GA-CQDs/CNN possessed multiple overlapping layers, which indicated that the 2D sheet structure of CNN and rGO were well maintained. Fig. 6 g was the HRTEM image of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. As shown, a distinguished interface between CNN and rGO was observed. It might indicate the formation of nanojunction, which probably resulted in more efficient electron transfer within the composite system [56].

The optical properties of the samples were studied by UV-vis absorption spectra (Fig. 7 a) and PL emission spectra (Fig. 7b). As shown in Fig. 7a, the absorption edge of CNN exhibited a blue-shift when compared with that of BCN. The reason was probably attributed to the well-known quantum confinement effect [22,57]. The incorporation of CQDs into CNN led to an improvement of the visible light absorption, and the result was in good agreement with previous reports [13,19]. Further enhancement in visible light absorption intensity could be observed for GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. This might be caused by the multiple reflections of incident light within the interconnected network of porous aerogel. Additionally, the band structure determined from the (ahv)2 versus photon-energy plots of BCN, CNN, CQDs/CNN and GA-CQDs/CNN-24% were 2.75 ev, 2.95 ev, 2.87 ev and 2.53 ev, respectively, as shown in the inset of Fig. 7a.

The separation and migration rate of the photo-induced carriers of the obtained samples were revealed by Photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 7b, both CNN and BCN showed a strong intrinsic fluorescence emission peak at 442 nm and 464 nm respectively, while CQDs/CNN exhibited a weak fluorescence emission spectrum peak at about 435 nm. This result revealed that CQDs played an important role in suppressing the recombination of the photo-generated carriers. For BCN, CNN and CQDs/CNN, a blue shift of the fluorescence emission peaks was observed. The result was consistent with that of UV-vis spectra. Remarkably, fluorescence quenching phenomenon was clearly observed in the PL emission spectrum of GA-CQDs/CNN-24%. This might be attributed to rGO which contacted intimately with CQDs/CNN could serve as a “highway” for the transfer of photo-generated electrons. And the unique 3D interconnected porous structure also played an important role in shorting the diffusion length of charge migration, thus hindered the charge recombination.

The electrochemical experiment was conducted to further investigate the photo-induced charge transfer and separation behaviors. The renewable photocurrent density during the five “on-off” irradiation cycles in Fig. 8 a declared the good stability of the samples. As shown, the transient photocurrent density of CQDs/CNN was more than 0.45 μA cm−2, whereas that of CNN was less than 0.35 μA cm−2. This result confirmed the effect of CQDs on promoting light harvest and electron separation as mentioned above. As expected, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% showed the highest photocurrent intensity (0.8 μA cm−2) which was almost 1.78, 2.29, and 2.50 times higher than that of CQDs/CNN, CNN and BCN (0.33 μA cm−2), respectively. This enhancement was probably due to the 3D interconnected network and nanojunction formed between rGO and CQDs/CNN. The GA-CQDs/CNN-24% had a significantly improved separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers and it has been proved by the weakest PL emission peak and the strongest intensity transient photocurrent. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 8b, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% had the smallest diameter of semicircular Nyquist curve of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy among all CN-based electrodes, this further confirmed the result. An obvious decrease for CQDs/CNN in semicircular Nyquist plot was also observed, which was attributed to the improved electronic conductivity with the effects of CQDs. Note that the BCN and CNN exhibited similar result in electrochemical experiment, this could be due to both of them suffered from fast recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes.

Adsorption property of the as-prepared photocatalysts were demonstrated by using MO as a model pollutant. As shown in Fig. 9 a, BCN was almost inactive toward the adsorption of MO. As for CNN and CQDs/CNN, their absorption rates were 15% and 16%, respectively. It was clear that all the GA-CQDs/CNNs displayed higher adsorption capacity when compared with CQDs/CNN. And among all the samples, GA-CQDs/CNN-40% displayed the highest adsorption removal (∼45%). Obviously, the larger surface area and abundant porous structures were contributed to the enhanced adsorption capacity, which was consistent with the change of SBET discussed earlier. To further investigate the adsorption capacity toward different dyes on the samples, the adsorption tests were extended to MB (Fig. S4). The adsorption capacity on MB changed in the order of GA-CQDs/CNNs > CNN > CQDs/CNN > BCN, GA-CQDs/CNN-40% still showed the highest adsorption rate (53%), which was consistent with the result of adsorption rate on MO.

Photocatalytic activities of the samples were evaluated by degradation of MO under visible-light irradiation. Before light irradiation, the mixed solution of dye and photocatalyst was stirred for 60 min to establish an adsorption‐desorption equilibrium. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the MO aqueous solution was very stable and the concentration was almost unchanged in the absence of catalyst under visible-light irradiation. Clearly, only 12% and 39% of MO was removed over BCN and CNN in 4 h. As for CQDs/CNN, an obvious increase of MO removal rate (around 65%) was observed under the identical conditions, owning to the positive effect of CQDs as we discussed in the previous section. Compared to CQDs/CNN, all the GA-CQDs/CNN composites had much higher MO removal rate under the identical conditions. As indicated in Fig. 10a and 10b, with increasing content of GO in GA-CQDs/CNN, although the adsorption capacity of GA-CQDs/CNN increased gradually, the photocatalytic activity decreased. Among all the GA-CQDs/CNN samples, GA-CQDs/CNN-8% exhibited the highest degradation rate whose reaction rate constant reached 0.503 h−1, while GA-CQDs/CNN-40% displayed the best adsorption capability. However, both of them are not the sample whose removal rate was the highest. In our experiment, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibited the highest MO removal rate of 91.1% within 4 h. This should be attributed to GO and CQDs/CNN that played different roles. The kinetic study exhibited that the photocatalytic degradation of MO over these samples belongs to the quasi-first-order kinetics. The reaction rate constant (k) of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was 0.485 h−1, which was about 18.7 and 7.7 times higher than that of BCN and CNN, respectively.

The photocatalytic stability is of much importance to practical applications. Hence, the cycling stability of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% was measured. As shown in Fig. 10c, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibited inappreciable loss of photocatalytic activity after four-cycle reaction processes, revealing the excellent stability of this kind of ternary photocatalyst.

Furthermore, to investigate the universality of GA-CQDs/CNN-24% ternary composite, photocatalytic degradation examinations towards MB was performed and the detailed results were presented in the Supporting Information (Fig. S2).

To determine the main active species in this photocatalytic process, the ESR spin-trap with DMPO technique was carried out. As shown in Fig. 11 a and Fig. 11b, when the samples were exposed to visible light, the typical signals of
O2 − and
OH were detected in water and in methanol solution, respectively, while no signal could be observed in dark. The ERS signal of
O2 − was much stronger than that of
OH under visible light. Because the VB of CNN is more positive than the energy level of HO− or H2O, the remaining photo-generated holes on the VB of CNN could not react with water to produce the
OH radicals. (Eθ(H2O/
OH) = 2.40 V, Eθ(OH−/
OH) = 1.99 V). The
OH radicals only generated from the process that O2
− radicals partially oxidized H2O. This result indicated that
OH played partial role during the photocatalytic process and O2
− was the main photocatalytic active groups. Additionally, the peak intensity of the h+ under light illumination was much stronger than that in the dark (Fig. 11c), revealing that h+ also played a vital role in the enhanced photocatalytic performance.

The photocatalytic enhancement of GA-CQDs/CNN could be ascribed to the stronger light absorption, the faster charge separation and the higher surface adsorption. On the one hand, CQDs/CNN was sensitive to visible light and CNN could generate abundant electron−hole pairs with the help of CQDs. On the other hand, an efficient separation of the electron−hole pairs occurred with the help of CQDs and rGO since both of them could act as the electro-reservoir. What is more, due to the unique porous structure, GA-CQDs/CNN possessed considerable adsorption capability for dye, which was kinetically favorable for the surface chemical reaction. Meanwhile, such porous structure was beneficial to light harvest. All mentioned above led to the enhanced photocatalytic activity.

On the basis of the above results, a tentative mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of GA-CQDs/CNN was proposed, which was illustrated in Scheme 2 . Here, when GA-CQDs/CNN was irradiated under visible light, CQDs could enhance light absorption greatly, and CNN generated much more electrons, then the photo-excited electrons transfered into the CB of CNN and the VB preserved the remaining holes. The accumulated electrons subsequently escape to the surface of CQDs and rGO simultaneously for O2 reduction. The electrons could produce O2
− by one-electron reduction (O2 + e− → O2
−, O2/O2
− = −0.33 eV) [58,59]. And the O2
− radicals would partially oxidize H2O to form
OH active species, others would together with
OH active species react with the organic dyestuff absorbed on the surface of rGO. It was noteworthy that given the VB of CNN was more positive than the energy level of HO− or H2O, the remaining photo-generated holes on the VB of CNN could not react with water to produce the
OH radicals. And the holes were consumed directly by oxidation of dyes. In order to further confirm the reaction mechanism we proposed, a radical trapping experiment was performed. The degradation effects of radicals and hole in the photo-degradation system was consistent with the results of ESR experiment and the mechanism we proposed above. The results of trapping experiment were discussed in detail in the Supporting Information (Fig. S3).

In conclusion, we have designed a new type of ternary 3D porous aerogel photocatalyst with rGO, CQDs and CNN by a two-step hydrothermal method. This GA-CQDs/CNN-x photocatalyst not only exhibits high visible light photocatalytic activity, but also possesses high adsorption capacity. Among all the GA-CQDs/CNN samples, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibits the highest removal rate for MO. The enhanced photocatalytic and adsorptive performance can be ascribed to the following factors. First, the 3D porous framework with large specific surface area can facilitate the light absorption and the reactant adsorption. Second, the large planar interface between rGO and CNN conld accelerate the transfer and separation of photoelectron. Third, the CQDs located on CNN can also greatly promote light harvest and electron separation. Furthermore, GA-CQDs/CNN-24% exhibits excellent recyclability stability for MO photodegradation. The corresponding photocatalytic degradation mechanism also interpreted both theoretically and experimentally. It is believed that such a well-designed approach may pave the way for preparing other 3D ternary photocatalysts with high photocatalytic activity and further utilizations in the field of environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Plan Project of Guangdong Province China [2016A010103047] and the National Natural Science Foundation of China – China [21476052].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.01.298.
